---
title: Dramatic Dilemmas
tags: [dilemmas, structure]
---

# Dramatic Dilemmas

A dilemma worth branching on has no comfortable third option — both
answers must cost the protagonist something real, or the choice reads
as a quiz rather than a decision. The strongest dilemmas are anchored
to a specific relationship: the reader is not choosing an abstract
policy, they are choosing what they are willing to do to, or for,
someone they have come to care about.

## Stakes must be legible before the choice

If the reader cannot articulate what each answer costs before they
commit, the choice is decorative. Establish the stakes of both answers
in the scene leading up to the commit beat, not after.

## Two answers, not two branches of tone

Both answers should be dramatically serious. A dilemma where one
answer is clearly the "good" path and the other is a joke option is not
a dilemma — it is a dare.
